topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official memo sat on my desk, crisp and offensively white. "Notice of Campus Closure." I picked it up, the cheap paper crinkling under my thumb. A slow, dry laugh escaped my throat, a sound devoid of humor. Four years. Four years of paying exorbitant tuition for a glorified brick and mortar institution that was clearly obsolete. I tossed the paper onto the pile of outdated syllabi, the sound barely audible over the hum of the fluorescent lights. I didn't bother reading the fine print; the reality was already etched in my gut—this whole enterprise was a sham, and now the curtain was falling. I stood up, stretching my arms above my head, feeling a strange lightness, like shedding a heavy, ill-fitting skin.

[story 2]

"So, the whole charade ends?" I muttered, leaning back in my chair until the worn vinyl squeaked in protest. My jaw tightened, the muscle jumping beneath my skin. I watched the receptionist across the hall, her practiced, saccharine smile seeming painfully brittle. It was tedious, this final performance. I didn't bother gathering my books. Why would I? They were just paper, relics of a system designed to funnel students into mediocrity. A deep, almost physical sigh escaped me, heavy with utter dismissal. I stood up, smoothing down my unnecessarily expensive sweater, and walked toward the door, not looking back at the imposing facade of the administration building.

[story 3]

The news hit me like a dull, unexpected thud. I stared at the screen, the cheerful graphics of the university logo suddenly seeming grotesque and mocking. I didn't feel sadness; only a profound sense of having wasted valuable time. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. "Of course," I whispered to the empty room, the sound raspy. This place had always felt like a gilded cage, and now the lock was finally breaking. I picked up my backpack, the weight suddenly feeling insignificant. There was no relief, just a cold, hard recognition that I had navigated a pointless maze, and now the exit was simply a door, waiting to be walked through without ceremony.
